coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical synthesis of FeNx doped carbon quantum dots for sensitive detection of Cu2+ ion
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Siyuan
Bao, Weijie
Yang, Fan
Yan, Xingru
Sun, Yang
Zhang, Ge
Yang, Wang
Li, Yongfeng
coredata.dc:description: A novel strategy was developed to fabricate FeNx-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe-N-CQDs) to detect Cu2+ ions selectively as a fluorescence probe. The Fe-N-CQDs were synthesized by an efficient electrolysis of a carbon cloth electrode, which was coated with monoatomic iron-anchored nitrogen-doped carbon (Fe-N-C). The obtained Fe-N-CQDs emitted blue fluorescence and possessed a quantum yield (QY) of 7.5%. An extremely wide linear relationship between the Cu2+ concentration and the fluorescence intensity was obtained in the range from 100 nmol L−1 to 1000 nmol L−1 (R2 = 0.997), and the detection limit was calculated as 59 nmol L−1. Moreover, the Fe-N-CQDs demonstrated wide range pH compatibility between 2 and 13 due to the coordination between pyridine nitrogen and Fe3+, which dramatically reduced the affection of the protonation and deprotonation process between H+ and Fe-N-CQDs. It is notable that the Fe-N-CQDs exhibited a rapid response in Cu2+ detection, where stable quenching can be completed in 7 s. The mechanism of excellent selective detection of Cu2+ was revealed by energy level simulation that the LUMO level of Fe-N-CQDs (−4.37 eV) was close to the redox potential of Cu2+, thus facilitating the electron transport from Fe-N-CQDs to Cu2+.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrolysis
FeNx-doped CQDs
Cu2+ detection
pH compatibility
Quick response

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+
Mechanism of carbon dots quenched by Cu2+
Possible mechanism of quick response to Cu2+
Determination of Cu2+ in real water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) AFM image of the as-prepared Fe-N-CQDs-2; (b) Height distribution of the Fe-N-CQDs-2; (c) The PL spectra of Fe-N-CQDs-2; (d) TEM image of Fe-N-CQDs-2.
(a) FTIR spectra of original Fe-N-C and Fe-N-CQDs-2; (b) XPS spectra of Fe-N-C and Fe-N-CQDs-2; (c)–(f) High resolution C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and Fe 2p XPS spectrum of the obtained Fe-N-CQDs-2.

(a)–(b) The selectivity of Fe-N-CQDs-2 to different metal ions and anions (1 mmol L−1) in the absence (black column) and presence (red or blue column); (c) Fluorescence spectra of Fe-N-CQDs-2 (150 μg mL−1) as the Cu2+ concentration increases; (d) The relationship between F0/F and the Cu2+ concentration, Inset: the linear relationship between F/F0 and the Cu2+ concentration.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of Fe-N-CQDs-2 in different pH conditions; (b) Fluorescence intensity of Fe-N-CQDs-2 affected by pH range.
(a) Time-resolved PL decay of Fe-N-CQDs-2; (b) Time-resolved PL decay of Fe-N-CQDs-2 and Cu2+; (c) Band gap calculation of N-CQDs; (d) Band gap calculation of Fe-N-CQDs-2.
The energy level simulation of Fe-N-CQDs-2.
(a) Fluorescence intensity and quenching time curve of Fe-N-CQDs-2; (b) Nyquist plots of Fe-N-CQDs, N-CQDs and CQDs in alkaline environment with a fitted equivalent circuit.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical synthesis of FeNx doped carbon quantum dots for sensitive detection of Cu2+ ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum, Changping, Beijing, 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum, Changping, Beijing, 102249, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors. State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum, 18# Fuxue Road, Changping, Beijing, 102249, China. Tel./fax: +86 10-8973-3477.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel strategy was developed to fabricate FeNx-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe-N-CQDs) to detect Cu2+ ions selectively as a fluorescence probe. The Fe-N-CQDs were synthesized by an efficient electrolysis of a carbon cloth electrode, which was coated with monoatomic iron-anchored nitrogen-doped carbon (Fe-N-C). The obtained Fe-N-CQDs emitted blue fluorescence and possessed a quantum yield (QY) of 7.5%. An extremely wide linear relationship between the Cu2+ concentration and the fluorescence intensity was obtained in the range from 100 nmol L−1 to 1000 nmol L−1 (R2 = 0.997), and the detection limit was calculated as 59 nmol L−1. Moreover, the Fe-N-CQDs demonstrated wide range pH compatibility between 2 and 13 due to the coordination between pyridine nitrogen and Fe3+, which dramatically reduced the affection of the protonation and deprotonation process between H+ and Fe-N-CQDs. It is notable that the Fe-N-CQDs exhibited a rapid response in Cu2+ detection, where stable quenching can be completed in 7 s. The mechanism of excellent selective detection of Cu2+ was revealed by energy level simulation that the LUMO level of Fe-N-CQDs (−4.37 eV) was close to the redox potential of Cu2+, thus facilitating the electron transport from Fe-N-CQDs to Cu2+.

The FeNx structure are reserved integrally in Fe-N-CQDs from Fe-N-C through a simple chemical oxidation method. Fe-N-CQDs-2 exhibits excellent selectivity to Cu2+, outstanding pH compatibility and fast response in metal ions detection. Energy level simulation and fluorescence lifetime detection reveal that the LUMO of Fe-N-CQDs are closer to the redox potential of Cu2+, leading to the non-radiative recombination between Fe-N-CQDs and Cu2+, which quench the fluorescence.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: With wide usage in industry, copper as a common heavy metal contaminant distributes widely in the form of Cu2+ in the ecological system such as rivers and groundwater, which will cause serious environmental pollution. According to the standard set by the World Health Organization (WHO), the maximum permissible limit of Cu2+ in drinking water should be no more than 1.0 mg L−1 [1]. At present, a series of conventional analytical techniques have been used in the detection of Cu2+, including fluorescence analysis [2], inductively coupled plasma spectroscopy [3], atomic absorption spectroscopy [4], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [5], anodic stripping voltammetry [6,7] infrared spectroscopy [8]. Compared with other methods, the fluorescence analysis based on fluorescence probe has some special advantages including its easy operation technique and minimal sample consumption [9]. However, some probes have high costs [10] and may be affected by the concentration of metal ions [11]. Therefore, it is meaningful to design a new kind of novel probe with low cost, convenience and stability.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as novel nanomaterials, have drawn great attention and been widespread used in electroluminescent light-emitting diodes [12], optical bioimaging [13], and chemical analysis [14]. Among them, on account of its excellent water solubility, outstanding fluorescence stability and selective fluorescence quenching caused by metal ions, carbon quantum dots exhibit superior property and irreplaceability in selective metal ions detection in water as fluorescence probe, which has been widely used to detect numerous metal ions including Cu2+ [15], Cr6+ [16,17], Fe3+ [18], Au3+ [19], Hg2+ [20,21] at a low concentration level. Although these CQDs offer excellent sensitivity to abundant metal ions, they still suffer either from slow response time or pH environment affection [15,22]. Liu obtained a nitrogen-doped CQDs with a response time of 10 min from hydrothermal treatment of grass [15]. Rong synthesized B and N co-doped CQDs to detect Cu2+, which response time was about 2 min [22]. Slow response speed will increase the test time, which will reduce the efficiency of metal ions detection. As for the pH compatibility of CQDs, some scholars have presented their ideas. Luo fabricated aryl-modified graphene quantum dots with stable PL properties in a wide pH range between 1 and 11 [23]. Yuan reduced the content of surface easily ionized functional groups such as –NH2 and –COOH to reduce the affection caused by H+ [24]. The above reports indicate that adjustment or modification of easily ionized functional groups can reduce the ionization of H+ on CQDs, which improves CQDs pH compatibility [23,24]. Property of fast response time and pH compatibility will enhance the detection capability of CQDs, making CQDs adapt to more complex detection environments. Therefore, it is meaningful to fabricate a kind of CQDs possessing excellent detection limit, quick response and pH compatibility for metal ions detection.

In our previous work, we introduced an electrolysis method for fabricating nitrogen-doped CQDs in which the N-C structure in the raw material could be reserved integrally [25]. In this work, we synthesized a novel FeNx-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe-N-CQDs) through this electrolysis method by electrolyzing monoatomic iron-anchored nitrogen-doped carbon (Fe-N-C), which exhibited an excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Cu2+ ions possessing a low-level detection limit (59 nmol L−1) and linear quantitation range from 100 nmol L−1 to 1000 nmol L−1 (R2 = 0.997). The obtained Fe-N-CQDs demonstrated excellent pH compatibility in a wide range from 1 to 13, and can be quenched steadily as fast as 7 s. Importantly, the Fe-N-CQDs performed reliably in the spiked tap water test, which is in outstanding agreement with the detection result from inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy. These results provided a theoretical basis for the development of CQDs with high sensitivity, pH compatibility and fast response properties.

CuCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, CaCl2, BaCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2, KCl, FeCl3·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3, CdCl2, NaOH, MgO, 1,10-phenanthroline and ethanol were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Corporation (Shanghai, China). The ethanol was of spectral grade, and the other chemicals were of analytical grade. All chemicals were used without further purification, and all aqueous solutions were prepared by deionized (DI) water.

Fe-N-C were synthesized as follows. First of all, to obtain the MgO template, 40 g MgO solid powder was mixed with 500 mL deionized water, and the mixture were boiled for 24 h in a reflux apparatus. After that, the above MgO mixture was calcined at 500 °C for 30 min to dewater thoroughly. Secondly, 1 g MgO template, 0.5 mmol FeCl3·6H2O solid and 1.5 mmol 1,10-phenanthroline were added into 100 mL ethanol and stirred for 12 h to form a mixture. Finally, the mixture was separated from ethanol by rotary evaporation, and pyrolyzed at 800 °C under argon for 2 h with a heating rate of 10 °C per minute. The obtained sample was picked with HCl and H2SO4 for 30 min to remove the MgO template and unstable Fe, then pickled with deionized water to remove the HCl. As the sample was filtered and dried, the Fe-N-C was obtained.

Fe-N-CQDs was synthesized by electrochemical oxidation of Fe-N-C coated on carbon cloth electrode. To obtain the electrode, the mixture of Fe-N-C and PTFE with ethanol were coated on the carbon cloth (3 cm × 3 cm). As the electrode was dried at 80 °C for 12 h, the electrode was used as the anode for DC electrolysis. To investigate the influence caused by PTFE content and voltage, different PTFE and voltage conditions were set, and the related samples were named Fe-N-CQDs-1 (9.9%, 5 V), Fe-N-CQD-2 (15%, 5 V), Fe-N-CQD-3 (20%, 5 V), Fe-N-CQD-4 (15%, 3 V) and Fe-N-CQD-5 (15%, 7 V), respectively. The electrolyte contained 8 mL H2O, 30 mg NaOH and 35 mL C2H5OH and the counter electrode was Pt foil. After 24 h, the yellow electrolyte was further dialyzed over DI water for 24 h in a dialysis bag (3500 Da). Finally, with the solution freeze-dried, the Fe-N-CQDs powder was obtained. The preparation process was shown in Scheme 1 .

The metal ions selectivity experiment was as follows: the Fe-N-CQDs powder was mixed with DI water and record fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm as initial intensity (F0) with 1 mL suspension of Fe-N-CQDs (150 μg mL−1). After that, different metal ions solutions (0.4 mL, 1 mmol L−1) such as Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, K+, Fe3+, Cr3+ and Cd2+ were added into the suspension of Fe-N-CQDs (0.6 mL, 150 μg mL−1). Specially, to inhibit the hydrolysis, a drop of HCl (0.1 mol L−1) was added into above metal ions and blank solutions. After several minutes, fluorescence intensity of fully shaken suspension was recorded as F at the same excitation wavelength. The experiment on the sensitive detection of Cu2+ was repeated by using different concentrations of Cu2+. Fluorescence spectra were repeatedly recorded at room temperature, and all the above experiments were carried out under the same conditions.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were recorded on a Tecnai G2 F20 with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Atomic force microscopes (AFM) images were collected with a Bruker Multimode 8. The fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were a Bruker Tensor II. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis (XPS) were performed on Thermo Fisher K-Alpha spectrometer with an exciting source of Al Kα. Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra (UV-vis) were measured by Shimadzu UV-2700. The concentration of Fe ions in Fe-N-C and Fe-N-CQDs were analyzed by an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, Optima 7300 V). The fluorescence emission spectra were measured on a Hitachi F7000. Fluorescence lifetime of the Fe-N-CQDs were measured by FLS-9800. The distribution of Fe in Fe-N-C was revealed by High-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM, Titan Cubed Themis G2 300 FEI 200 kV).

The QY of Fe-N-GQDs was measured according to following Eq. (1), and quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 was used as reference (QY = 0.54 at 360 nm).
(1)
Φ x
=
Φ st
(
K x
/
K st
)
(
η x
/
η st
)
2
where, Ф is the quantum yield, K is slope, which belongs to linear fitting line of the integral area of fluorescence intensity versus absorbance. η is the refractive index of solution, x and st stand for Fe-N-CQDs sample and quinine sulfate, respectively.

The band gap of Fe-N-GQDs was estimated according to the following Eq. (2).
(2)
(
A h ν
K
)
1 / 2
= h ν −
E g
where, A is the absorbance of the UV-vis. The hv is 1240/wavelength and K is the product of sample concentration and thickness of the cuvette. E
g is the band gap of Fe-N-GQDs.

The molecular orbital energy level of Fe-N-GQDs was estimated according to the following Eqs. (3) and (4).
(3)
E LUMO
= − e
(
E Red
+ 4.4
)
(4)
E HOMO
=
E LUMO
−
E g
where, E LUMO is the lowest unfilled molecular orbital energy level of Fe-N-CQDs. E HOMO is the highest filled molecular orbital energy level of Fe-N-CQDs. E Red is the value of reduction potential measured by voltammetry.

First of all, the high-angle annular dark-filed scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) was used to reveal the distribution of Fe in Fe-N-C (Fig. S1), which evidenced that Fe species exist as single-atomic-sites on the surface of Fe-N-C. Furthermore, Fe-N-C was also characterized by extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) technique (Fig. S2). Compared with characteristic peaks of Fe foil and FePc (phthalocyanine iron), characteristic peaks of Fe-N-C show that FeNx structure was integrated into the carbon framework, which confirmed the Fe-N bond in the Fe-N-C structure. The Fe-N-CQDs were synthesized by one step electrolysis of Fe-N-C coating carbon cloth electrode, and the preparation process for Fe-N-CQDs is shown in the Scheme 1.

The AFM images showed that all Fe-N-CQDs samples are well separated from each other in a form of quasi-spherical structure (Fig. 1 a and b and Fig. S3). In the Fe-N-CQDs fabrication process, we found that the PTFE usage amount and electrolytic voltage are two important factors for affection the diameter and optical properties of Fe-N-CQDs, which were characterized by AFM and PL (Table S1, Fig. 1c and Fig. S4). In the synthesized Fe-N-CQDs, we found the Fe-N-CQDs-2 possess the strongest fluorescence intensity (850 nm) with small diameter size of 4.86 ± 0.26 nm, which was synthesized in the electrolytic voltage of 5 V and 15 wt% binder of PTFE. Furthermore, the quantum yield of Fe-N-CQDs-2 was measured to be 7.5% as shown in Fig. S5. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of Fe-N-CQDs-2 are obtained at 340 and 440 nm, respectively (Fig. 1c), which shows a typical excitation-dependent feature. It can be attributed to the distribution of different surface states and the functional groups of Fe-N-CQDs-2. The TEM image of Fe-N-CQDs-2 was also showed that it exhibits uniformly spherical structure with a mean size of about 5.0 nm (Fig. 1d), which is consistent with the AFM detection. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (insert in Fig. 1d) revealed that the Fe-N-CQDs-2 has good crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) lattice planes of graphitic carbon [26]. In addition, the synthesized Fe-N-CQDs-2 had stable fluorescence, which retained over 95% of original value after storing 7 days in solution (Fig. S6). The above results demonstrated that a kind of stable carbon quantum dots with highly graphitized structure is successfully synthesized by electrolysis method.

Furthermore, FT-IR spectroscopy was used to investigate the change of chemical structure between Fe-N-C and Fe-N-CQDs-2 as shown in Fig. 2 a. Both Fe-N-C and Fe-N-CQDs-2 possess absorption bands of N-H/O-H stretching vibration (3080-3785 cm−1), CO/C-N stretching vibration (1651 cm−1), C-O/C-O-C stretching vibration (1092 cm−1), N-H bending vibration (1538 cm−1) and C-H bending vibration (667 cm−1). Compared with Fe-N-C, the peaks of Fe-N-CQDs-2 in the region of 2924, 2853 and 865 cm−1 are attributed to vibrations of C-H group, and the peaks of carboxyl (1651 cm−1) and hydroxide groups (1092 cm−1) increase obviously. The new peak at 1382 cm−1 manifest the generation of new –NO2 group [27]. These results imply that hydroxide, carboxyl, nitro and amino groups are generated on the Fe-N-CQDs-2, which enhanced the stability in aqueous system and increased the number of chelating sites.

The structure of Fe-N-CQDs-2 was further characterized via XPS (Table S2). The spectra presented in Fig. 2b shows typical peaks of C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (400 eV), O 1s (532 eV) and Fe (710 eV), respectively. The high-resolution region of C 1s (Fig. 2c) displays four characteristic peaks located at 284.6, 285.1, 286.2 and 288.6 eV, which are corresponding to the sp2 graphitic CC structure, C-N, C-O and CO, respectively. Obviously, there is a clear enhanced signal for CO compared to raw Fe-N-C (Fig. S7a), which indicates that a mass of oxygen-containing functional groups is obtained on the surface of Fe-N-CQDs-2. The high-resolution region of O 1s (Fig. 2d) shows that Fe-N-CQDs-2 contains CO (531.2 eV) and C-O-C/C-OH (532.4 eV) chemical bonds. Compared to raw Fe-N-C (Fig. S7b), the significant enhancement of CO bond reconfirms the exist of oxygen-containing functional groups in the structure of the Fe-N-CQDs-2, which is consistent with C 1s spectrum. In addition, the high-resolution region of N 1s (Fig. 2e) shows that three peaks at 398.9, 400.0 and 406.7 eV are assigned to C-N, Fe-N [28] and –NO2 groups, respectively. Compared to N 1s spectrum of Fe-N-C (Fig. S7c), the existence of –NO2 groups in Fe-N-CQDs-2 can be attributed to the etching and oxidation of chemical scissors (∙OH) during electrolysis, which is agreement with the FT-IR results. The C-N bond at 398.9 eV illustrates the major state of the doped nitrogen was pyridinic N. In addition, the existence of C-N and Fe-N bonds demonstrate the FeNx structure in the defect or edge area of graphitized structure of the carbon dots. The Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 2f) consists of two characteristic peaks at 710.0 eV and 724.0 eV, and the peak at 710.0 eV is assigned to the FeNx structure [29]. Both peaks of Fe-N in Fe-N-C (Figs. S7c and S7d) and Fe-N-CQDs-2 (Figs. 2e and 3f) illustrate that FeNx structure is reserved in Fe-N-CQDs-2 when etched from Fe-N-C electrode. In addition, the ICP-OES result confirmed that Fe was etched in Fe-N-CQDs (0.41 wt%) from Fe-N-C successfully, which is consistent with the results of XPS (Table S3). These results not only suggested that the hydroxide, carboxyl, nitro and amino functional groups on is generated on the surface of Fe-N-CQDs-2, but also indicated that the structure of FeNx is reserved from Fe-N-C integrally.

The UV-Vis absorption spectra exhibit a significant absorbance at 230 and 283 nm (Fig. S8), which indicates the existence of π-π∗ and n-π∗ electronic transition. Compared with N-CQDs, an enhanced absorption band ranging from 260 to 310 nm was observed in the absorption spectrum of Fe-N-CQDs-2 under same concentration condition, which can be attributed to metal-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) caused by the electron transformation from Fe to N in Fe-N-CQDs-2 [9,30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of Fe-N-CQDs-2 to Cu2+ ions, comparative experiments were carried out with Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, K+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Cr3+ metal cations and HCO3 2-, Br−, CO3 2-, PCO3 2-, Cl−, NO3 -, SO4 2- anions. Considering that low metal ions concentration showed slight influence on fluorescence quenching and metal ions concentration are usually at low levels less than 1 mmol L−1 in real condition, we set metal ions selectivity experiment with the concentration at 1 mmol L−1 to ensure the accuracy of the experiment. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, the fluorescence of Fe-N-CQDs-2 only quenched by Cu2+ obviously, while the influence on fluorescence quenching caused by other metal cations and anions was negligible. These results confirmed the excellent selectivity of Fe-N-CQDs-2 to Cu2+. Furthermore, to evaluate the sensitivity of Fe-N-CQDs-2 to Cu2+ ions, the emission fluorescence intensity of Fe-N-CQDs-2 against different concentration of Cu2+ (0–1000 μmol L−1) was shown in Fig. 3c. With the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the emission peak of Fe-N-CQDs-2 at 340 nm decreased gradually, which suggests the great sensitivity to Cu2+. Fig. 3d displays a great linearity range from 100 to 1000 nmol L−1 (R2 = 0.997), and the detection limit of Fe-N-CQDs-2 is calculated to be 59 nmol L−1. These results indicate that Fe-N-CQDs possess not only great sensitivity in Cu2+ detection (Table S4) [22,31–37], but also excellent potential in the application of Cu2+ detection.

The pH compatibility is important for carbon quantum dots in its application for metal ions detection in complex acid and base environment. As known to all, the electronic-rich nitrogenous groups are important for providing delocalized electron-hole recombination states of carbon quantum dots, which are susceptible to the effects of protonation and deprotonation [38]. We speculate that these effects to nitrogenous groups will decrease by the modification of FeNx structure, which the pH compatibility of Fe-N-CQDs-2 will enhance. To detect the pH compatibility of Fe-N-CQDs-2, the pH response experiment was carried out with the pH value between 1 and 13 as shown in Fig. 4 a and b. The fluorescence intensity of Fe-N-CQDs-2 shows more than 90% of the maximum fluorescence intensity in a wide pH value range from 4 to 10, meanwhile it even keeps about 80% of the maximum fluorescence intensity when pH varies in pH values of 2 and 13. Compared with N-CQDs synthesized in our previous work [25], Fe-N-CQDs-2 showed the strong pH compatibility in metal ions detection. The fluorescence of Fe-N-CQDs-2 could also be quenched obviously by Cu2+ in different pH value conditions (Fig. S9), which further confirmed its strong pH compatibility. Meanwhile, Fe-N-CQDs still have stable fluorescence after stored in acid medium for a long term (Fig. S10). These results indicate that the FeNx structure enhances the pH compatibility of Fe-N-CQDs due to reduction of the affection on protonation and deprotonation possess, which the lone electron pair of nitrogenous groups can transfer into the empty d orbital of iron [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of carbon dots quenched by Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of Fe-N-CQDs-2 caused by the presence of Cu2+, fluorescence lifetime detection was performed. The time of fluorescence decay decreased from 4.28 ns to 3.65 ns after addition of Cu2+ (Fig. 5 a and b), which illustrates the existence of dynamics quenching that Fe-N-CQDs-2 colliding with Cu2+ would transfer its excited state electrons to the empty 3d orbitals of Cu2+ and occurring non-radiative recombination [40].

The possible mechanism of selectivity is proposed to explain by energy level simulation. In our previous work, we found that the N-CQDs synthesized in same chemical oxidation method possessed excellent performance in Fe3+ detection, which LUMO and band gap was calculated as −4.29 eV and 2.83 eV, respectively (Fig. 5c) [25]. The FeNx structure in Fe-N-CQD-2 widen the band gap to 3.36 eV (Fig. 5d), which overlaps the redox potential of Cu2+ (1.98 eV) [41]. At the same time, the LUMO of the Fe-N-CQD-2 was calculated as −4.37 eV, which is approximate to the Cu2+ ions redox potential instead of Fe3+. Therefore, the electrons in LUMO of Fe-N-CQDs-2 transfer to Cu2+ more easily instead of HOMO, which Fe-N-CQDs-2 could be quenched by Cu2+ (Fig. 6 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of quick response to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The quick response of Fe-N-CQDs-2 to Cu2+ was confirmed by fluorescence quenching time experiment as shown in Fig. 7 a. The fluorescence intensity of Fe-N-CQDs-2 quenched fast to a stable low level within 7 s after adding Cu2+, which indicates that Fe-N-CQDs-2 have the capacity of quick response in Cu2+ detection. Additionally, the Nyquist diagrams for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements of Fe-N-CQDs were carried out to further study the reason for quick response. The results indicate that Fe-N-CQDs has excellent electrical conductivity than comparation samples in Fig. 7b. Based on dynamics quenching mechanism, the fluorescence quenching is caused by electrons transformation between Fe-N-CQDs and Cu2+. At the same time, with the electron density increasing caused by Fe, the reduction of Fe-N-CQDs resistance can speed up the dynamics quenching of Fe-N-CQDs-2 with Cu2+, which results in quick response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ in real water
ce:section.ce:para: With great performance in Cu2+ detection of aqueous solution, further detection performance of Fe-N-CQDs-2 in real water solution was investigated under same conditions. Concentration of Cu2+ measured by linear equation (100-1000 nmol L−1, R2 = 0.997) is similar to the results of ICP with RSD (Relative standard deviation) less than 5%, which confirms that Fe-N-CQDs-2 synthesized by this chemical oxidation method has excellent performance in Cu2+ detection (Table S5).

In conclusion, we developed a brand-new FeNx-doped carbon quantum dots as a probe for the selective detection of Cu2+ through an electrolysis method, where Fe-N-CQDs was etched from Fe-N-C with FeNx structure. The obtained Fe-N-CQDs contained rich functional groups such as alkaline pyridine nitrogen, nitro, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and FeNx structure, which possessed an average particle size of 4.86 ± 0.26 nm with superior fluorescence performance (QY = 7.5%). Determination of Cu2+ could be completed at different concentrations with a linear range of 100–1000 nmol L−1 and a low-level detection limit of 59 nmol L−1. More importantly, the easily ionized –NH2 groups modified by FeNx structure could reduce the H+ effect, which endowed superior pH compatibility to Fe-N-CQDs. With the increase of free electrons concentration, the stable quenching of Fe-N-CQDs in the detection of Cu2+ could be completed in 7 s. With energy level simulation, the doping of Fe adjusted the LUMO of Fe-N-CQDs (−4.37 eV) approximate to the Cu2+ ions redox potential, which made Fe-N-CQDs selective to Cu2+ in metal ions detection. This work fabricated a new type of carbon quantum dots with superior pH stability and quick response for efficient Cu2+ detection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully thank for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21776302 and 21776308), and the Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum, Beijing (No. 2462020YXZZ033).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gee.2021.04.005.
